title: Keeping AI Under Control

modules.title: Why AI Safety Is Everyone's Problem
modules.sub: Understanding why AI risk is a shared societal concern
modules.description: Learners examine why AI safety extends far beyond researchers and engineers, touching industries, governments, and everyday life. They assess common misconceptions about who bears responsibility for safe AI development and begin building a personal framework for understanding the stakes involved.

modules.title: What Alignment Actually Means
modules.sub: Unpacking the core concept behind safe AI behavior
modules.description: Learners break down the concept of alignment — what it means for an AI system to reliably pursue human-intended goals — using plain language and concrete examples. They distinguish between narrow and broad alignment challenges and evaluate why this problem is harder to solve than it first appears.

modules.title: When AI Systems Misbehave
modules.sub: Exploring real cases where AI produced harmful or wrong outputs
modules.description: Learners analyze documented examples of AI systems behaving in unexpected or harmful ways, from biased hiring tools to dangerous content generation. They identify the conditions that allowed each failure to occur and consider what earlier intervention points could have looked like.

modules.title: Reward Hacking and Unintended Goals
modules.sub: Seeing how optimizing for the wrong target goes wrong
modules.description: Learners explore the phenomenon of reward hacking — where an AI finds shortcuts to score well on a metric while completely missing the intended objective. They work through vivid thought experiments and real examples to understand why specifying goals precisely is one of the hardest problems in AI design.

modules.title: Human Oversight in AI Systems
modules.sub: Examining how humans stay meaningfully in the loop
modules.description: Learners evaluate the mechanisms organizations use to maintain human control over AI systems, including monitoring, intervention triggers, and audit trails. They weigh the trade-offs between automation efficiency and oversight rigor, and consider what meaningful human control actually requires in practice.

modules.title: Catastrophic Risk: Hype vs Reality
modules.sub: Separating credible long-term risks from science fiction fears
modules.description: Learners critically assess the spectrum of catastrophic risk scenarios associated with advanced AI, from plausible near-term failures to more speculative existential concerns. They apply a structured framework to distinguish evidence-based risks from hype, and calibrate their own position on where serious attention is warranted.

modules.title: Who Is Working on AI Safety
modules.sub: Mapping the global landscape of researchers, labs, and policymakers
modules.description: Learners survey the key actors in the AI safety ecosystem — including academic institutions, AI labs, nonprofits, and regulatory bodies — and understand what each is working on and why. They compare different philosophical and strategic approaches to safety and consider how these efforts do or do not align with one another.

modules.title: What You Can Do Right Now
modules.sub: Translating awareness into concrete, practical next steps
modules.description: Learners identify actions they can take in their own professional context to support safer AI development and deployment, regardless of their role or technical background. They leave with a short personal action plan, a curated set of resources for continued learning, and clarity on how to advocate for responsible AI practices in their organization.

synopsis: AI safety is one of the most debated topics in tech, yet most people have never encountered a clear, jargon-free explanation of what it means. This course demystifies alignment, risk, and the global effort to keep powerful AI beneficial.
rationale: With a demand score of 6/10 and only 0.60 corpus similarity to the future-focused 'Previsión del Progreso en IA', an accessible AI safety course addresses a rapidly growing public interest that current curriculum leaves almost entirely unexplored.
